title: practical english usage - Part 177
content:
We   may want to emphasise that both (or all) parts of a coordination have equal (n)either      ... (n)or;  not    only 228 emphatic       coordination:        both      ... and; He drinks too much,  but at least he doesn’ t smoke as well.

You can have an apple, but you can’t have an orange too.

point by using not... also, not... as well or not... too.

After mentioning a positive (non-negative) fact or idea, we  can add a negative 3 positive      + negative:      also     / as   welt     / too not (      ...and you can't have an orange also/as well/too.) You can’ t have an apple, and you can’ t have an orange either.

Also, as well and too are not normally used with not in this way.

For details of this and similar structures, ►    309.2.

I can't sing and nor can any of my family.

Daniel isn't here today. Neither is Jack.

Another way of expressing this is with neither/nor + be/auxiliary + subject.

Daniel isn’ t here today. Jack isn’ t here either. (     Jack isn't here neither.) idea or fact in another clause with not... either.

After using a negative verb in one clause, we  can mention a second negative 2 two    separate       negative       clauses:     not    either For the use of neither... nor to join two negative ideas (e.g. He neither smokes nor drinks), ►    228.3.

independent labour union. (More normal than a lack of... and. ..) The country suffered from a lack of employment legislation or of an This also happens after negative nouns or adjectives.

(     The project was not successful in political terms and in economical terms ) The project was not successful in economic terms or in political terms.

And cannot normally be used in this kind of structure.

Note that neither cannot be used in this way.

She didn't phone that day, nor the next day.

Our  main need is not food, nor money. It is education.

verb, adjective, etc.

However, we  can use nor after a pause, to separate and emphasise a second She wasn't angry or upset. It's not on the table or in the cupboard.

He doesn't smoke or drink. (     He doesn't smoke nor drink.) usually join them with or.

When  not (used once) refers to two or more verbs, nouns, adjectives, etc, we 1  not    used    once    for   two    negative       ideas 227 not    .. . o r,  not    . nor,    and     not   ___________ Conjunctions, Sentences and ClausesSection 20 grammar •  228  emphatic coordination: both ... and; (n)either... (n)or, not only We go there not only in winter, but also in summer.

but also can go immediately before the words or expressions that they modify.

In the rather formal structure not only .. . but also, the expressions not only and 4 not    only     .. . but    also neither smiled, spoke nor looked at me). This rule is mistaken.

says that neither should not introduce more than two alternatives (as in He As with either... or, there is a prescriptive 'rule', found in some books, which Neither James nor Virginia were at home, (less formal) Neither James nor Virginia was at home, (normal) singular, but it can be plural in a less formal style.

When singular subjects are connected by neither. .. nor, the verb is normally but not He neither smiled ... nor. . . (          Neither he smiled...) Neither cannot begin a complete clause in this structure.

He neither smiled, spoke, nor looked at me.

Sometimes more than two ideas are connected by neither. .. nor.

The film was neither well made nor well acted.

/ neither smoke nor drink, (less formal: I don't smoke or drink.) This structure is the opposite of both ... and. It is usually rather formal.

3 neither       ...  nor For either as a determiner, ►     156.   For pronunciation, ►     156.6.

vanilla). This rule is mistaken: it does not correspond to normal usage.

not introduce more than two alternatives (as in either raspberry, lemon or There is a prescriptive 'rule', found in some books, which  says that either should You'll either leave this house or I'll call the police.

You can either have tea or coffee. He's either in London or New York.

However, unbalanced sentences with either... or are common.
